title: 6. Machine Learning with Random Survival Forests
text: classical models like Cox Proportional Hazards model highly interpretable, rely strict assumptions linear, additive effects. Random Survival Forests (RSF) overcome building non-linear ensemble decision trees. tutorial, demonstrate run standalone RSF using SuperSurv’s unified wrappers, evaluate individual performance, finally include Super Learner ensemble.

title: 9. Causal Effects and Adjusted Marginal Contrasts (RMST)
text: clinical trials observational studies, researchers often wish compare survival outcomes two groups. Historically, answered using Hazard Ratio (HR) Cox Proportional Hazards model. However, HR non-collapsible—meaning omission unmeasured covariates mathematically bias effect toward null—strictly relies proportional hazards assumption. survival curves cross, HR becomes mathematically invalid. SuperSurv solves evaluating group differences absolute time scale using Restricted Mean Survival Time (RMST) via G-computation (Standardization) top Ensemble Super Learner. RMST calculates area survival curve specific time horizon, τ\tau. comparing expected RMST everyone dataset belonged Group 1 versus everyone belonged Group 0, obtain robust, absolute measure difference: ΔRMST=E[Y(1)]−E[Y(0)]=RMSTGroup 1(τ)−RMSTGroup 0(τ) \Delta \text{RMST} = E[Y(1)] - E[Y(0)] = \text{RMST}_{\text{Group 1}}(\tau) - \text{RMST}_{\text{Group 0}}(\tau)

title: 9. Causal Effects and Adjusted Marginal Contrasts (RMST)
text: interpret ΔRMST\Delta \text{RMST} depends entirely nature exposure variable. math G-computation identical , statistical terminology must used responsibly. Interpretation: “Administering drug causally adds average 4.2 months life 5-year period compared placebo.” Interpretation: “adjusting baseline clinical covariates, presence biomarker marginally associated 4.2 additional months survival 5-year period.”

what: 2. Estimate the Adjusted Marginal RMST Contrast
title: 9. Causal Effects and Adjusted Marginal Contrasts (RMST)
text: use estimate_marginal_rmst() function compute adjusted marginal contrast RMST scale. function sets binary grouping variable x4 1 patients, predicts survival curves, integrates predictions restriction horizon tau, repeats procedure x4 set 0. difference two standardized averages yields adjusted RMST contrast. Interpretation: resulting Δ\DeltaRMST value -1.24, indicates , standardizing observed covariate distribution using fitted Super Learner ensemble, group x4 = 1 predicted approximately 1.24 fewer months restricted mean survival group x4 = 0 100-month horizon. Uncertainty: quantify uncertainty, estimate_marginal_rmst() can optionally apply perturbation-based inference procedure conditional fitted ensemble. returns perturbation-based standard error, confidence interval, Wald-type p-value. Note: perturbation procedure conditions final fitted SuperSurv model refit learner library ensemble weights, resulting confidence interval reflects conditional uncertainty standardized RMST contrast may relatively narrow.
code: # Estimate the adjusted difference up to tau = 100 months results <- estimate_marginal_rmst(   fit = fit,    data = metabric,    trt_col = "x4",    times = new.times,    tau = 100 ) #> Adjusted Delta RMST at tau = 100: -1.27 time units    print(results$ATE_RMST) #> [1] -1.269855 rmst_results_inf <- estimate_marginal_rmst(   fit = fit,   data = metabric,   trt_col = "x4",   times = new.times,   tau = 100,   inference = TRUE,   B = 100,   seed = 123 ) #> Adjusted Delta RMST at tau = 100: -1.27 time units | SE = 0.026 | 95% CI = [-1.32, -1.219]  rmst_results_inf$ATE_RMST #> [1] -1.269855 rmst_results_inf$SE_RMST #> [1] 0.02571039 rmst_results_inf$CI_RMST #>     lower     upper  #> -1.320246 -1.219464 format.pval(rmst_results_inf$p_value, digits = 3, eps = 1e-16) #> [1] "<1e-16"

title: Extending SuperSurv
text: following learner ignores covariates fits weighted Kaplan-Meier curve. intentionally simple, satisfies full SuperSurv() learner contract. can test wrapper outside ensemble adding library: custom learner can mixed built-learners:
code: my.surv.km <- function(time, event, X, newdata, new.times,                        obsWeights = NULL, id = NULL, ...) {   if (is.null(obsWeights)) {     obsWeights <- rep(1, length(time))   }    fit_km <- survival::survfit(     survival::Surv(time, event) ~ 1,     weights = obsWeights   )    step_surv <- stats::stepfun(fit_km$time, c(1, fit_km$surv), right = FALSE)   surv_probs <- step_surv(new.times)    pred <- matrix(     surv_probs,     nrow = nrow(newdata),     ncol = length(new.times),     byrow = TRUE   )    fit <- list(object = fit_km)   class(fit) <- "my.surv.km"    list(pred = pred, fit = fit) }  predict.my.surv.km <- function(object, newdata, new.times, ...) {   fit_km <- object$object   step_surv <- stats::stepfun(fit_km$time, c(1, fit_km$surv), right = FALSE)   surv_probs <- step_surv(new.times)    matrix(     surv_probs,     nrow = nrow(newdata),     ncol = length(new.times),     byrow = TRUE   ) } data("metabric", package = "SuperSurv") dat <- metabric[1:40, ] x_cols <- grep("^x", names(dat))[1:3] X <- dat[, x_cols, drop = FALSE] times <- seq(50, 150, by = 50)  wrapper_out <- my.surv.km(   time = dat$duration,   event = dat$event,   X = X,   newdata = X[1:5, , drop = FALSE],   new.times = times )  dim(wrapper_out[["pred"]]) #> [1] 5 3 dim(predict(wrapper_out[["fit"]], newdata = X[1:5, , drop = FALSE], new.times = times)) #> [1] 5 3 fit <- SuperSurv(   time = train$duration,   event = train$event,   X = X_train,   newdata = X_test,   new.times = seq(50, 300, by = 50),   event.library = c("surv.coxph", "my.surv.km"),   cens.library = c("surv.coxph"),   control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE) )  event_weights(fit)

title: 5. Advanced Hyperparameter Tuning & Grid Search
text: single machine learning configuration rarely optimal every dataset. Random Forest deep trees might overfit small data, shallow trees might underfit complex data. Super Learner framework, handle “tuning” adding multiple versions algorithm library, different hyperparameters. meta-learner automatically evaluates via cross-validation assigns highest weight best configuration. SuperSurv provides create_grid function automate process entirely.

title: 2. Model Performance & Benchmarking
text: SuperSurv ensemble trained, must rigorously prove outperforms individual base learners. survival data involves right-censoring, use standard classification metrics like simple accuracy. Instead, evaluate model across three critical dimensions: 1. Calibration: predicted survival probability match actual observed survival rate? 2. Discrimination (AUC & C-index): Can model correctly rank patient survive longer? 3. Overall Accuracy (Brier Score): combined measure calibration discrimination. tutorial demonstrates extract metrics visualize benchmark comparisons using SuperSurv’s built-evaluation suite.

title: 2. Model Performance & Benchmarking
text: begin loading metabric dataset defining evaluation time grid (new.times). Crucial Methodological Note: new.times grid never exceed maximum observed follow-time training cohort. example, training data spans 1 100 days, predicting survival day 150 extrapolation. Non-parametric models (like Survival Trees) mathematically extrapolate, parametric models (like Weibulls) generate highly unreliable tail estimates. Always bind evaluation grid within limits observed data!
code: library(SuperSurv) library(survival)  data("metabric", package = "SuperSurv") set.seed(123) train_idx <- sample(1:nrow(metabric), 0.7 * nrow(metabric)) train <- metabric[train_idx, ] test  <- metabric[-train_idx, ]  X_tr <- train[, grep("^x", names(metabric))] X_te <- test[, grep("^x", names(metabric))]  # Our max follow-up is well beyond 200, so this grid is safe. new.times <- seq(50, 200, by = 25)

title: 7. Parametric Survival Models
text: Cox model semi-parametric (leaves baseline hazard unspecified), fully parametric models assume survival times follow specific mathematical distribution, Weibull, Exponential, Log-Normal distribution. Parametric models statistically powerful produce perfectly smooth survival curves. However, highly brittle: assume data follows Weibull distribution, true biological hazard completely different shape, model heavily biased. SuperSurv acts safety net. can include multiple parametric assumptions library. parametric assumption perfectly matches data, SuperSurv give high weight. assumption wrong, cross-validation risk spike, SuperSurv safely assign weight zero.

title: 7. Parametric Survival Models
code: library(SuperSurv) library(survival)  data("metabric", package = "SuperSurv") set.seed(42)  train_idx <- sample(1:nrow(metabric), 0.7 * nrow(metabric)) train <- metabric[train_idx, ] test  <- metabric[-train_idx, ]  X_tr <- train[, grep("^x", names(metabric))] X_te <- test[, grep("^x", names(metabric))] new.times <- seq(50, 200, by = 25)  # Define a library covering different parametric assumptions parametric_library <- c("surv.coxph",       # Semi-parametric baseline                         "surv.weibull",     # Assumes hazard increases/decreases monotonically                         "surv.exponential", # Assumes constant hazard over time                         "surv.lognormal")   # Assumes hazard rises then falls

title: 7. Parametric Survival Models
text: run ensemble exactly . Internally, SuperSurv fit Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) models map continuous survival predictions onto discrete new.times evaluation grid.
code: fit_parametric <- SuperSurv(   time = train$duration,   event = train$event,   X = X_tr,   newdata = X_te,   new.times = new.times,   event.library = parametric_library,   cens.library = c("surv.coxph"),   control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE),   verbose = FALSE,   selection = "ensemble",   nFolds = 3 )

title: 7. Parametric Survival Models
text: Let’s look cross-validated risks final meta-learner weights.
code: summary(fit_parametric) #> Summary of SuperSurv fit #>   Selection: ensemble  #>  #> Call: #> SuperSurv(time = train$duration, event = train$event, X = X_tr,  #>     newdata = X_te, new.times = new.times, event.library = parametric_library,  #>     cens.library = c("surv.coxph"), verbose = FALSE, control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE),  #>     selection = "ensemble", nFolds = 3) #>  #> Event ensemble: #>                      learner weight   risk status #>        surv.coxph_screen.all 0.9979 8.8384     ok #>      surv.weibull_screen.all 0.0000 8.8420     ok #>    surv.lognormal_screen.all 0.0021 8.8444     ok #>  surv.exponential_screen.all 0.0000 8.8508     ok #>  #> Censoring ensemble: #>                learner weight   risk status #>  surv.coxph_screen.all      1 1.0488     ok #>  #> Predictions: 572 observations x 7 times #> Evaluation times: 7 values from 50 to 200  #> Elapsed time (seconds): #> everything      train    predict  #>      2.058      1.936      0.118

title: 7. Parametric Survival Models
text: Look closely weights assigned surv.exponential. Exponential distribution assumes risk event (hazard) completely constant time. real-world cancer datasets like metabric, assumption almost always false (risk usually increases time peaks shortly surgery). Exponential assumption fits data poorly, cross-validated risk high, SuperSurv smartly assign weight 0.000.00. including parametric models SuperSurv library, allow data—researcher—dictate mathematical distributions actually appropriate patient cohort!

title: 10. Scaling Up with Parallel Processing
text: Double Super Learner incredibly powerful statistical framework, computationally demanding. define 5 base algorithms use 10-fold cross-validation, SuperSurv fit minimum 50 separate machine learning models just Event ensemble, plus another set Censoring ensemble! default, R executes models sequentially (one ). However, SuperSurv natively supports parallel processing using modern future future.apply ecosystem. allows distribute cross-validation folds across multiple CPU cores, dramatically reducing computation time.

title: 10. Scaling Up with Parallel Processing
text: use parallel processing, need future future.apply packages installed.
code: install.packages(c("future", "future.apply"))

title: 10. Scaling Up with Parallel Processing
text: SuperSurv relies define parallel “plan” running function. gives complete control many resources package allowed consume.
code: library(SuperSurv) library(future) library(survival)  data("metabric", package = "SuperSurv")  # 1. Define the parallel plan # 'multisession' opens background R sessions.  # We tell it to use 4 CPU cores (workers). plan(multisession, workers = 4)

title: 4. High-Dimensional Data & Variable Screening
text: modern clinical research bioinformatics, common encounter high-dimensional datasets number predictors (genes, biomarkers, clinical features) far exceeds number observed events (p≫np \gg n). Feeding thousands raw variables directly complex machine learning algorithms introduces two massive problems: 1. Computational Bottlenecks: Algorithms like Random Survival Forests become prohibitively slow. 2. Overfitting: Models mathematically memorize background noise instead true biological signals, destroying --sample prediction accuracy. SuperSurv elegantly handles Variable Screening. Screening acts computational filter models fitted. evaluates predictors, drops statistical noise, passes highly associated features base learners.

title: 4. High-Dimensional Data & Variable Screening
text: demonstrate screening works, use standard metabric dataset, artificially inject 100 columns pure random noise. simulates high-dimensional genomic dataset measured features absolutely relationship patient survival.
code: library(SuperSurv) library(survival)  data("metabric", package = "SuperSurv") set.seed(42)  # Create 100 columns of random noise (simulating irrelevant genes/biomarkers) n_patients <- nrow(metabric) noise_data <- matrix(rnorm(n_patients * 20), nrow = n_patients) colnames(noise_data) <- paste0("noise_", 1:20) # Combine the real data with the noise high_dim_data <- cbind(metabric, noise_data)  # Standard Train/Test Split train_idx <- sample(1:nrow(high_dim_data), 0.7 * nrow(high_dim_data)) train <- high_dim_data[train_idx, ] test  <- high_dim_data[-train_idx, ]  X_tr <- train[, grep("^x|^noise", names(high_dim_data))] X_te <- test[, grep("^x|^noise", names(high_dim_data))] new.times <- seq(50, 200, by = 25)

title: 4. High-Dimensional Data & Variable Screening
text: Just library prediction algorithms, SuperSurv utilizes library screening algorithms. can pair prediction model screening function create streamlined pipeline. SuperSurv includes comprehensive suite built-screeners: * screen.marg: Marginal screening (keeps variables strong univariate associations). * screen.glmnet: Penalized Lasso screening (shrinks irrelevant feature coefficients exact zero). * screen.elasticnet: Elastic Net screening (combines Lasso Ridge penalties; highly recommended highly correlated biological features like gene expression data). * screen.rfsrc: Tree-based screening (keeps features high Random Forest variable importance). * screen.var: Unsupervised screening (drops features near-zero variance). * screen.: baseline passthrough (keeps variables without screening). Let’s build library tests different combinations prediction screening:
code: # We pass a list of vectors. The first element is the prediction model,  # and the second element is the screening algorithm.  my_screen_library <- list(   c("surv.coxph", "screen.all"),         # Baseline: Cox model with ALL variables   c("surv.coxph", "screen.marg"),        # Screen by marginal association, then fit Cox   c("surv.weibull", "screen.elasticnet"),# Screen via Elastic Net, then fit Weibull   c("surv.rpart", "screen.var")          # Drop zero-variance noise, then fit a Tree )  # For the censoring library, we use an unscreened approach  cens_library <- c("surv.coxph")

title: 4. High-Dimensional Data & Variable Screening
text: pass paired library SuperSurv, meta-learner handles cross-validation absolute statistical rigor. Crucial Methodological Detail: screening step performed inside cross-validation folds. screen entire dataset splitting folds, cause “data leakage,” leading overly optimistic benchmark metrics. SuperSurv protects automatically.
code: fit_highdim <- SuperSurv(   time = train$duration,   event = train$event,   X = X_tr,   newdata = X_te,   new.times = new.times,   event.library = my_screen_library, # Pass our screened library   cens.library = cens_library,   control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE),   verbose = T,   nFolds = 3 )

SuperSurv solves using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations). title: 3. Ensemble vs. Best Model Selection
text: theoretical framework Super Learning, two distinct ways utilize cross-validated risks base algorithms: Ensemble Super Learner: Calculates weighted average (convex combination) base learners. “soft selection” smooths variance, integrates different feature spaces, generally yields lowest finite-sample prediction error. “Best” Model Selector: Identifies single algorithm lowest cross-validated risk assigns weight 1.0 (others 0.0). “hard selection” “winner-take-” approach. Ensemble asymptotically optimal, selecting single best model incredibly useful interpretability strictly tied one specific algorithm family. Instead manually cherry-picking best model—introduces researcher bias invalidates post-selection inference—SuperSurv automates selection using rigorous, internal cross-validation. can easily toggle two paradigms using selection argument.

title: 3. Ensemble vs. Best Model Selection
text: load metabric dataset define evaluation time grid exactly previous tutorials.
code: library(SuperSurv) library(survival)  data("metabric", package = "SuperSurv") set.seed(42)  # Standard Train/Test Split train_idx <- sample(1:nrow(metabric), 0.7 * nrow(metabric)) train <- metabric[train_idx, ] test  <- metabric[-train_idx, ]  X_tr <- train[, grep("^x", names(metabric))] X_te <- test[, grep("^x", names(metabric))] new.times <- seq(50, 200, by = 25)  # Define a diverse library of base learners my_library <- c("surv.coxph", "surv.weibull", "surv.rpart")

title: 3. Ensemble vs. Best Model Selection
text: fit two separate SuperSurv models. first use default Ensemble approach (selection = "ensemble"), second use winner-take-approach (selection = "best").
code: # 1. The Ensemble Super Learner (Weighted Average) fit_ensemble <- SuperSurv(   time = train$duration,   event = train$event,   X = X_tr,   newdata = X_te,   new.times = new.times,   event.library = my_library,   cens.library = my_library,   control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE),   verbose = FALSE,   selection = "ensemble",     # <-- Calculates fractional weights   nFolds = 3 )  # 2. The 'Best' Super Learner (Winner-Take-All) fit_best <- SuperSurv(   time = train$duration,   event = train$event,   X = X_tr,   newdata = X_te,   new.times = new.times,   event.library = my_library,   cens.library = my_library,   control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE),   verbose = FALSE,   selection = "best",         # <-- Selects the single best model   nFolds = 3 )

text: run main SuperSurv engine, important understand key parameters. Super Learner algorithm relies cross-validation assign weights base models, must model event censoring mechanism avoid biased evaluations. complete guide arguments need pass:

title: 1. SuperSurv with Ensemble
text: time: numeric vector observed follow-times training cohort. event: numeric vector indicating status observed time (typically 1 = event occurred, 0 = right-censored). X: data.frame matrix containing predictor variables (covariates) training set. include time event columns ! newdata: (Optional) data.frame covariates validation test set. provided, SuperSurv immediately generate predictions patients training phase, saving step. new.times: numeric vector defining exact time points want survival probabilities predicted (e.g., seq(50, 200, = 25)).

title: 1. SuperSurv with Ensemble
text: event.library: character vector base algorithms used predict actual survival outcome (e.g., "surv.coxph", "surv.rpart"). cens.library: library used estimate censoring mechanism time. SuperSurv uses predictions calculate Inverse Probability Censoring Weights (IPCW). can use exact library event models, simpler one.

title: 1. SuperSurv with Ensemble
text: "brier" (Default): Optimizes weights minimizing IPCW Brier score. Excellent overall prediction accuracy. "logloss": Optimizes weights minimizing negative log-likelihood. Excellent improving hazard discrimination. nFolds: number cross-validation folds used train meta-learner. V = 5 V = 10 standard. cross-validation prevents ensemble overfitting base learners. control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE): tells engine save fitted base learners final object. required want use predict() method new patients later. verbose: Set TRUE print progress messages console. Highly recommended large datasets complex libraries can track cross-validation progress. Let’s fit two models verbose = FALSE see meta-learner choice affects final ensemble weights.
code: # Fit 1: Least Squares Meta-learner fit_ls <- SuperSurv(   time = train$duration,   event = train$event,   X = X_tr,   newdata = X_te,                 # Predict on the test set immediately   new.times = new.times,       # Our evaluation time grid   event.library = my_library,   cens.library = my_library,   metalearner = "brier",    control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE),    verbose = T,             # Turn to TRUE in practice to see progress!   selection = "ensemble",   nFolds = 5                   # 5-fold CV for the meta-learner )  # Fit 2: Negative Log-Likelihood Meta-learner fit_nll <- SuperSurv(   time = train$duration,   event = train$event,   X = X_tr,   newdata = X_te,   new.times = new.times,   event.library = my_library,   cens.library = my_library,   metalearner = "logloss",       # Swap to nloglik   control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE),    verbose = FALSE,   selection = "ensemble",   nFolds = 5 )

what: A Unified Framework for Machine Learning Ensembles in Survival Analysis
title: A Unified Framework for Machine Learning Ensembles in Survival Analysis
text: SuperSurv R package building, evaluating, interpreting ensemble models right-censored survival data. core, package implements Super Learner-style ensemble framework continuous-time survival prediction right censoring. Using inverse probability censoring weighting (IPCW), combines heterogeneous base learners minimizing cross-validated prediction risk. framework supports learners return full survival curves well learners return risk scores, calibrated common survival-probability scale shared evaluation time grid. Beyond ensemble fitting, SuperSurv provides tools : hyperparameter tuning high-dimensional screening time-dependent model benchmarking SHAP-based interpretability covariate-adjusted restricted mean survival time (RMST) contrasts package also provides user-friendly model interface print(), summary(), coef(), exported accessors event_weights(), censor_weights(), learner_names(), training_variables(), selected_variables(), eval_times().

code: data("metabric", package = "SuperSurv") dat <- metabric[1:40, ] x_cols <- grep("^x", names(dat))[1:3] X <- dat[, x_cols, drop = FALSE] newX <- X[1:10, , drop = FALSE] times <- seq(50, 150, by = 50)  fit <- surv.coxph(   time = dat$duration,   event = dat$event,   X = X,   newdata = newX,   new.times = times,   obsWeights = rep(1, nrow(dat)),   id = NULL )  eval_brier(   time = dat$duration[1:10],   event = dat$event[1:10],   S_mat = fit[["pred"]],   times = times ) #> $brier_scores #> [1] 0.08094384 0.23312677 0.24225036 #>  #> $ibs #> [1] 0.1973619 #>  #> $times #> [1]  50 100 150 #>

code: data("metabric", package = "SuperSurv") dat <- metabric[1:80, ] x_cols <- grep("^x", names(dat), value = TRUE)[1:5] X <- dat[, x_cols, drop = FALSE] new.times <- seq(20, 120, by = 20)  fit <- SuperSurv(   time = dat$duration,   event = dat$event,   X = X,   newdata = X,   new.times = new.times,   event.library = c("surv.coxph", "surv.glmnet"),   cens.library = c("surv.coxph"),   control = list(saveFitLibrary = TRUE) )  tau_grid <- seq(40, 120, by = 20) plot_marginal_rmst_curve(   fit = fit,   data = dat,   trt_col = "x4",   times = new.times,   tau_seq = tau_grid,   inference = TRUE,   B = 100,   seed = 123 ) #> Adjusted Delta RMST at tau = 40: -0.124 time units | SE = 0.007 | 95% CI = [-0.137, -0.11] #> Adjusted Delta RMST at tau = 60: -0.544 time units | SE = 0.023 | 95% CI = [-0.59, -0.499] #> Adjusted Delta RMST at tau = 80: -1.29 time units | SE = 0.044 | 95% CI = [-1.376, -1.204] #> Adjusted Delta RMST at tau = 100: -2.141 time units | SE = 0.059 | 95% CI = [-2.257, -2.025] #> Adjusted Delta RMST at tau = 120: -3.183 time units | SE = 0.077 | 95% CI = [-3.333, -3.033]
